questions.question: Explain the difference between vectored and non-vectored interrupts with examples.
keyPoints: Vectored interrupts provide the address of the ISR directly to the CPU (e.g., x86 INT instruction with vector)
Non-vectored interrupts require CPU to poll the interrupt controller to find the device (older systems)
Vectored is faster as no polling needed
Non-vectored uses a single handler that identifies the source

questions.question: Describe the daisy chain interrupt priority scheme with its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Devices connected in series, closer to CPU has higher priority
First device requesting gets priority, signals propagate through chain
Advantage: Simple hardware implementation, no priority encoder needed
Disadvantage: Fixed priority, device failure can block entire chain, more delay for distant devices

questions.question: Explain the operation of an interrupt controller in managing multiple device interrupts.
keyPoints: Receives multiple IRQ signals from devices
Uses priority encoder to determine highest priority request
Masks or buffers lower priority requests
Sends INTR signal to CPU and provides interrupt vector on INTA
Allows CPU to handle one interrupt at a time efficiently

questions.question: What is interrupt latency? Discuss factors that affect interrupt latency in a computer system.
keyPoints: Interrupt latency = time from interrupt request to start of ISR execution
Factors: Current CPU instruction completion, interrupt enable/disable state, priority of interrupt vs current execution
Hardware factors: interrupt controller delay, bus arbitration time
Lower latency is critical for real-time systems
Can be reduced by priority schemes and hardware optimization

questions.question: Explain the difference between level-triggered and edge-triggered interrupts with use cases.
keyPoints: Level-triggered: interrupt active as long as level is high, requires device to lower line to de-assert
Edge-triggered: triggered by transition (0→1 or 1→0), doesn't depend on level
Level-triggered: can cause repeated service if not handled (needs masking)
Edge-triggered: better for devices that can't hold request line, more precise
Use case: Level for devices with persistent status, edge for one-shot events

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in handling an interrupt from the initial request to returning to the main program.
keyPoints: 1. Device raises IRQ line
2. Interrupt controller receives and prioritizes
3. Controller raises INTR to CPU
4. CPU completes current instruction, saves state (PC, PSW)
5. CPU acknowledges with INTA, receives vector
6. Looks up vector in interrupt vector table
7. Jumps to ISR and executes handler
8. ISR completes, executes IRET instruction
9. CPU restores saved state
10. Returns to interrupted program

questions.question: Explain the concept of interrupt nesting. Under what circumstances should nesting be allowed or prevented?
keyPoints: Nesting allows higher priority interrupts to preempt lower priority ISR execution
Requires saving sufficient state (all registers) on stack
Should allow: for time-critical high-priority events like hardware failures
Should prevent: when ISR is not re-entrant, shared resources need protection, to avoid stack overflow
Controlled via interrupt enable/disable flags in ISR

questions.question: Draw and explain the block diagram of an interrupt system showing CPU, interrupt controller, and peripheral devices. Include the signal lines and data flow.
keyPoints: Block diagram showing: Multiple devices → IRQ lines → Interrupt Controller → INTR to CPU + INTA from CPU + Data bus for vector
Explain each signal: IRQ (device to controller), INTR (controller to CPU), INTA (CPU to controller), Data bus for vector
Role of priority encoder in controller
Interrupt vector table in memory
CPU fetches ISR address from vector table
Show complete signal flow and timing

questions.question: Explain priority encoding in interrupt handling. Design a 4-input priority encoder truth table and explain how it determines the highest priority interrupt.
keyPoints: Priority encoder: combinational logic that encodes highest priority active input
For 4 inputs (IRQ3-IRQ0), IRQ3 has highest priority, IRQ0 lowest
Truth table showing all 16 combinations and 2-bit binary output
Logic: if IRQ3=1 output=11, else if IRQ2=1 output=10, etc.
Explain how this reduces latency by eliminating need for polling
Can be expanded for more inputs (8→3 bit, 16→4 bit)

questions.question: Compare polling and interrupt-based I/O. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other? Discuss the trade-offs in terms of CPU utilization, response time, and hardware complexity.
keyPoints: Polling: CPU continuously checks device status registers
Interrupt: Device notifies CPU when service needed
CPU Utilization: Polling wastes cycles on idle devices, interrupts are efficient
Response Time: Polling can be faster for very frequent events, interrupts have latency overhead
Hardware: Polling simpler (no interrupt controller), interrupts need additional hardware
Choose polling: simple systems, predictable high-frequency events, real-time requirements
Choose interrupt: multiple slow devices, unpredictable events, power-sensitive systems

questions.question: Explain the working of the Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC) with reference to Intel 8259A. Discuss cascaded mode operation and interrupt acknowledge cycle.
keyPoints: 8259A PIC: manages 8 IRQ lines, can be cascaded for 16 lines
Internal registers: IRR (Interrupt Request Register), ISR (In-Service Register), IMR (Interrupt Mask Register)
Cascaded mode: one PIC as master, one as slave, IRQ2 used for cascade
Priority: rotating and fixed modes supported
INTA cycle: CPU sends INTA, PIC places vector on data bus
Vector format: 8-bit with upper/lower 5 bits programmable
Explain the two INTA pulses and data transfer
